questions.question: Explain the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Preemptive allows forced CPU removal, non-preemptive lets process run to completion
Preemptive enables better response time for interactive processes
Example of preemptive: Round Robin, SRTF
Example of non-preemptive: FCFS, non-preemptive priority

questions.question: Define the following scheduling criteria: (i) CPU utilization (ii) Throughput (iii) Turnaround time
keyPoints: CPU utilization: percentage of time CPU is busy
Throughput: number of processes completed per unit time
Turnaround time: total time from arrival to completion
Formula: Turnaround = Completion - Arrival time

questions.question: Consider three processes P1, P2, P3 with burst times 24, 3, and 3 ms respectively. Calculate average waiting time using SJF scheduling.
keyPoints: Execute P2 (3ms), then P3 (3ms), then P1 (24ms)
Waiting time P1 = 3+3 = 6 ms
Waiting time P2 = 0 ms
Waiting time P3 = 3 ms
Average waiting time = (6+0+3)/3 = 3 ms

questions.question: What is the convoy effect in FCFS scheduling? How does it affect system performance?
keyPoints: Convoy effect occurs when short processes wait behind long processes
Causes poor CPU utilization and high average waiting time
Short processes get delayed significantly
System becomes inefficient with mixed burst times

questions.question: Explain the concept of aging in CPU scheduling. How does it help in solving the problem of starvation?
keyPoints: Aging gradually increases priority of waiting processes over time
Prevents starvation by ensuring low-priority processes eventually get CPU
Implementation: Increment priority at regular intervals
Example: After waiting 5 seconds, increase priority by 1

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of Round Robin scheduling?
keyPoints: Advantages: Fair allocation, good response time, handles interactivity well
Disadvantages: Higher context switching overhead, performance depends on quantum size
If quantum too small: excessive context switches
If quantum too large: degenerates to FCFS

questions.question: Differentiate between multilevel queue scheduling and multilevel feedback queue scheduling.
keyPoints: Multilevel queue: fixed separate queues with no movement between them
Multilevel feedback queue: processes can move between queues based on behavior
Feedback queue prevents starvation and provides better adaptivity
Feedback queue is more complex to implement

questions.question: Consider four processes with the following arrival times and burst times: P1(0,7), P2(2,4), P3(4,1), P4(5,6). Calculate average waiting time and average turnaround time using (a) FCFS (b) SJF (non-preemptive).
keyPoints: FCFS order: P1, P2, P3, P4
FCFS Waiting: P1=0, P2=5, P3=7, P4=7, Avg=19/4=4.75
FCFS Turnaround: P1=7, P2=9, P3=8, P4=13, Avg=37/4=9.25
SJF order (by burst): P3(1), P2(4), P4(6), P1(7)
SJF Turnaround: P3=1, P2=5, P4=11, P1=18, Avg=35/4=8.75
SJF gives better results in this case

questions.question: Explain the Round Robin scheduling algorithm with a time quantum of 4 units. Consider processes P1(10), P2(5), P3(8) arriving at time 0. Show the execution timeline and calculate turnaround time for each process.
keyPoints: Time quantum = 4 units
P1 completes at 22, P2 at 8, P3 at 20
Average turnaround = 50/3 = 16.67
Waiting times: P1=12, P2=3, P3=12

questions.question: Discuss the various CPU scheduling criteria. How does the choice of scheduling algorithm affect each criterion?
keyPoints: CPU Utilization: Higher is better, depends on algorithm and system load
Throughput: Processes completed per unit time
Turnaround Time: Total time from arrival to completion
Waiting Time: Time spent in ready queue (minimization is key)
Response Time: First CPU response for interactive systems
FCFS: Good throughput, poor waiting/response for short jobs
SJF: Best average waiting/turnaround, but impractical
Round Robin: Good response time, fair but higher overhead
Priority: Depends on priority values, starvation possible

questions.question: What is priority scheduling? Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and how aging can be used to overcome starvation.
keyPoints: Priority scheduling assigns numerical priority to each process
Higher priority processes execute first
Can be preemptive or non-preemptive
Advantages: Important processes get immediate attention
Disadvantages: Starvation of low-priority processes, may cause deadlock
Aging solution: Gradually increase priority of waiting processes
Example: Increase priority by 1 for every 10 seconds of waiting
This ensures no process waits indefinitely for CPU
